### kernl
kernl — multi-agent orchestration runner

Usage:
  kernl [--config <path>] [--port <port>] <subcommand> [args...]

Subcommands:
  triage       What needs attention right now, in one call
  serve        Start the HTTP API server (add --no-orchestrator for GUI-only)
  doctor       Run system checks (env, binaries, config)
  epic         Manage epics (bead graphs)
  bead         Manage individual beads (run one locally, or read and edit the tracker)
  sweep        Close epics whose PRs are merged in master
  bookmark     Manage bookmarks
  capture      Capture a quick note/idea into the inbox (text arg or stdin)
  plan         Show the vault notes relevant to a topic (substrate-aware planning)
  capabilities Print the machine-readable CLI contract (JSON)
  robot-docs   Print the agent handbook (paste-ready, generated from metadata)
  version      Print version and build information
  task         Manage tasks (the GUI's task board, from the shell)
  project      Manage projects (list, create, edit, delete)
  note         Read and write vault notes (the same files the editor edits)
  inbox        Triage the capture inbox (list, add, process, classify)
  memory       Inspect and edit what the DA remembers (claims and telos)
  graph        Read the knowledge graph (nodes, edges, search, relatedness)
  settings     Read and write kernl.yaml through the running server
  health       Check that the server is up and answering (no subcommand needed)
  approval     See and resolve the gates waiting on a human
  session      Poke a running agent session
  ingest       Feed documents into the graph and triage what they extracted

Flags:
  --config, -c       Path to kernl.yaml (default: kernl.yaml; accepts --config=path)
  --port, -p         Server port (default: from kernl.yaml, or 8080)
  --server <url>     Address of the running server the GUI-parity verbs call
                     (default: http://127.0.0.1:<port>; env: KERNL_SERVER)
  --no-orchestrator  Serve only the GUI/graph/notes; do not require bd
  --version, -v      Print version and build information
  --help, -h         Show help for kernl or any verb/sub-verb

Exit codes:
  0  success
  1  runtime/internal error (backend, config, network, agent run)
  2  usage error (unknown verb/flag, missing argument, bad value, or a refused --yes gate)

Automation:
  kernl capabilities       machine-readable contract (JSON)
  kernl robot-docs guide   agent handbook
  --json                   on epic list, plan, doctor, version, and every
                           task/project/note/inbox subcommand

Run 'kernl <subcommand> --help' (or 'kernl help <subcommand>') for details.

### kernl triage
kernl triage — What needs attention right now, in one call

Usage:
  kernl triage [--json]

Answers "what should I do now" without five separate reads. Reports, in
the order you would act on them: captures waiting to be processed, ingest
reviews, beads in flight, beads ready to start, open tasks, approvals, and
whether the server is healthy. Each section names the command that shows
the rest.

A section that cannot be read says so and names why; it does not report
zero. "Nothing pending" and "could not ask" are different answers, and
conflating them is how a caller concludes no judgment gate is waiting.

Exit code is non-zero only when NOTHING could be read — a partial report
is a successful triage.

Flags:
  --json  Emit the whole report as one document, every section carrying
          {available, reason, count, items, command}

### kernl serve
kernl serve — Start the HTTP API server (add --no-orchestrator for GUI-only)

Usage:
  kernl [--config <path>] [--port <port>] serve [--no-orchestrator]

Flags:
  --port, -p         Server port (default: from kernl.yaml, or 8080)
  --no-orchestrator  Serve only the GUI/graph/notes; do not require bd

The server hosts the web GUI, the REST API and the SSE event streams.

### kernl doctor
kernl doctor — Run system checks (env, binaries, config)

Usage:
  kernl [--config <path>] doctor [--json]

Checks that bd, the agent CLI, Go and the config file are usable.
Exit code is non-zero when a required check fails.

Flags:
  --json  Emit {"ok","checks":[...],"recommendedAction"} on stdout

### kernl epic
kernl epic — Manage epics (bead graphs)

Usage:
  kernl epic <list|run|merge|abort|events|sessions> [args...]

Subcommands:
  list           List epics with child counts and state
  run            Execute an epic's bead graph in parallel
  merge          (Re-)run only the epic-level integration stages
  abort          Close an epic and its children; clean worktrees and agent state
  events         Print an epic's orchestration events
  sessions       List the agent sessions the server has open

Run 'kernl epic <subcommand> --help' for details on each.

'events' and 'sessions' read an epic's history from a running 'kernl serve';
the others drive the orchestrator locally.

### kernl epic list
kernl epic list — List epics with child counts and state

Usage:
  kernl epic list [--json]

Flags:
  --json  Emit {"epics":[{"id","title","children","state"}]} on stdout

### kernl epic run
kernl epic run — Execute an epic's bead graph in parallel

Usage:
  kernl epic run [--workflow <path>] [--autonomous] [--interactive] <epic-id>

Flags:
  --workflow <path>  Use a custom workflow YAML instead of the default profile
  --autonomous       Let the DA infer the workflow shape without prompting
  --interactive      With --autonomous: confirm the inferred shape first

### kernl epic merge
kernl epic merge — (Re-)run only the epic-level integration stages

Usage:
  kernl epic merge <epic-id>

Drives the epic bead through integration -> integration_review -> shipment.
Use it to recover a blocked epic; it does not run the children.

### kernl epic abort
kernl epic abort — Close an epic and its children; clean worktrees and agent state

Usage:
  kernl epic abort [--dry-run] <epic-id> --yes

Destructive: closes the epic and every child bead, removes their worktrees
and purges agent state. Requires --yes; use --dry-run to preview.

### kernl epic events
kernl epic events — Print an epic's orchestration events

Usage:
  kernl epic events <epic-id> [--follow] [--limit <n>] [--timeout <dur>] [--json]

The route is an SSE stream, not a paged list: it replays the hub's buffer
for the epic and then stays open. So by default this drains the replay
buffer and exits once the stream falls quiet — what a script wants — and
--follow keeps it open instead, like 'tail -f'.

Flags:
  --follow         Keep streaming as events arrive (exit with Ctrl-C)
  --limit <n>      Stop after n events
  --timeout <dur>  Give up after a duration (e.g. 30s, 2m)
  --json           One compact JSON event per line (NDJSON — a stream has
                   no last element to close an array with)

### kernl epic sessions
kernl epic sessions — List the agent sessions the server has open

Usage:
  kernl epic sessions <epic-id> [--json]

The epic id is required by the route but the server answers with every
active session in the process, not only that epic's — the session manager
does not index by epic.

  --json  Emit the API's session array verbatim

### kernl bead
kernl bead — Manage individual beads (run one locally, or read and edit the tracker)

Usage:
  kernl bead <run|list|get|create|set|close|rollback|refine-scope|mark-terminal> [args...]

Subcommands:
  run            Drive a single bead through its workflow
  list           List every bead in the registered repo
  get            Show one bead
  create         Create a bead
  set            Change fields of an existing bead
  close          Close a bead, recording why
  mark-terminal  Force a bead into a terminal state, skipping its workflow
  rollback       Rewind a bead to an earlier workflow state
  refine-scope   Rewrite a bead's description, notes or acceptance criteria

'bead run' drives an agent in a local worktree and needs the orchestrator
toolchain. Every other subcommand reads or edits the tracker through a
running 'kernl serve'.

### kernl bead run
kernl bead run — Drive a single bead through its workflow

Usage:
  kernl bead run <bead-id>

### kernl bead list
kernl bead list — List every bead in the registered repo

Usage:
  kernl bead list [--json]

The API serves the whole tracker: GET /api/beads takes no filters, so
there is nothing to scope by here. Filter downstream (--json | jq).

### kernl bead get
kernl bead get — Show one bead

Usage:
  kernl bead get <bead-id> [--json]

--json emits the API's full bead object; without it, the fields worth
reading on a terminal are printed.

### kernl bead create
kernl bead create — Create a bead

Usage:
  kernl bead create <title> [--type <t>] [--priority <n>] [--labels <a,b>] [--json]

Text flags: --description, --type, --assignee, --due, --acceptance,
--notes, --parent <bead-id>, --profile <id>, --workflow <id>.
Integers: --priority, --estimate. List: --labels <a,b,c>.
--json emits the created bead verbatim.

Invariants are not settable here: they are structured objects, not a flag.

Example:
  kernl bead create "wire the SSE reconnect" --parent ep-3 --priority 2

### kernl bead set
kernl bead set — Change fields of an existing bead

Usage:
  kernl bead set <bead-id> [--title <t>] [--state <s>] [--priority <n>] [--json]

At least one field is required; only the flags you pass are sent.

Text flags: --title, --description, --type, --state, --assignee, --due,
--acceptance, --notes, --parent, --profile.
Integers: --priority, --estimate.
Labels: --labels adds, --set-labels replaces the whole set, --remove-labels drops.
--json emits the updated bead verbatim.

### kernl bead close
kernl bead close — Close a bead, recording why

Usage:
  kernl bead close <bead-id> [--reason <text>] --yes [--json]

Requires --yes: closing a bead is what unblocks its dependents, so the
orchestrator may immediately dispatch agents and create worktrees for them.
Without --yes the close is described and nothing is sent.
--reason <text> is stored on the bead.

### kernl bead mark-terminal
kernl bead mark-terminal — Force a bead into a terminal state, skipping its workflow

Usage:
  kernl bead mark-terminal <bead-id> --state <state> [--reason <text>] --yes [--json]

Requires --yes: this abandons every workflow stage the bead had left —
review, integration, shipment — without running them. Use it to correct a
bead the orchestrator cannot finish, not to advance one.
--state is required; --reason is appended to the bead's notes.

### kernl bead rollback
kernl bead rollback — Rewind a bead to an earlier workflow state

Usage:
  kernl bead rollback <bead-id> --state <state> [--reason <text>] --yes [--json]

Requires --yes: rewinding discards the progress recorded after the target
state, and re-running those stages re-spawns agents.
--state is required; --reason records why the correction was needed.

The bd backend does not implement rewind — against a bd-backed server this
route fails loud. The verb exists for backends that do.

### kernl bead refine-scope
kernl bead refine-scope — Rewrite a bead's description, notes or acceptance criteria

Usage:
  kernl bead refine-scope <bead-id> [--description <t>] [--notes <t>] [--acceptance <t>] [--json]

At least one of the three is required. Each one you pass replaces that
field wholesale.

### kernl sweep
kernl sweep — Close epics whose PRs are merged in master

Usage:
  kernl sweep [--yes | --dry-run] [--repo <path>] [--failure-threshold <n>] [--backoff-minutes <a,b,...>] [--stale-warn-days <n>]

Lists epics awaiting PR review, asks gh whether each PR merged, and closes
the merged ones (epic + children) in the tracker.

Without --yes this is a dry-run preview: nothing is closed.

Flags:
  --yes                     Actually close the merged epics
  --dry-run                 Preview without closing (default behavior)
  --repo <path>             Repo to sweep (default: current directory)
  --failure-threshold <n>   Consecutive failures before backing off
  --backoff-minutes <list>  Comma-separated backoff schedule, e.g. 5,15,60
  --stale-warn-days <n>     Warn when a PR is open longer than n days

### kernl bookmark
kernl bookmark — Manage bookmarks

Usage:
  kernl bookmark <add|import> [args...]

Subcommands:
  add            Add a bookmark by URL (archives the page HTML)
  import         Bulk-import bookmarks from an export file

### kernl bookmark add
kernl bookmark add — Add a bookmark by URL (archives the page HTML)

Usage:
  kernl bookmark add <url>

### kernl bookmark import
kernl bookmark import — Bulk-import bookmarks from an export file

Usage:
  kernl bookmark import <pocket|pinboard> <file>

### kernl capture
kernl capture — Capture a quick note/idea into the inbox (text arg or stdin)

Usage:
  kernl capture [--json] [--] <text> | echo <text> | kernl capture

Prints the created capture's ID; --json (first arg only) emits
{"id","status"} instead.

Examples:
  kernl capture "call the accountant tomorrow"
  echo "idea: robot mode for the CLI" | kernl capture
  kernl capture -- --help   (captures the literal text "--help")

### kernl plan
kernl plan — Show the vault notes relevant to a topic (substrate-aware planning)

Usage:
  kernl plan [--json] <topic>

Flags:
  --json  Emit {"topic","notes":[{"title","via","snippet"}]} on stdout

Example:
  kernl plan "caching strategy"

### kernl capabilities
kernl capabilities — Print the machine-readable CLI contract (JSON)

Usage:
  kernl capabilities [--json]

Emits every verb, flag, env var and exit code as JSON, plus a
contractVersion agents can pin against. Output is JSON with or without
the flag.

### kernl robot-docs
kernl robot-docs — Print the agent handbook (paste-ready, generated from metadata)

Usage:
  kernl robot-docs guide

### kernl version
kernl version — Print version and build information

Usage:
  kernl version [--json]

Flags:
  --json  Emit {"version","commit","built","go"} on stdout

### kernl task
kernl task — Manage tasks (the GUI's task board, from the shell)

Usage:
  kernl task <list|create|set|delete> [args...]

Subcommands:
  list           List tasks, optionally scoped to one project
  create         Create a task
  set            Change fields of an existing task
  delete         Delete a task and its companion note

Talks to a running server over the REST API, so 'kernl serve' must be up
(or point elsewhere with --server <url> / KERNL_SERVER).

Run 'kernl task <subcommand> --help' for details on each.

### kernl task list
kernl task list — List tasks, optionally scoped to one project

Usage:
  kernl task list [--project <project-id>] [--json]

Flags:
  --project <id>  Only tasks belonging to that project
  --json          Emit the API's task array verbatim (camelCase)

### kernl task create
kernl task create — Create a task

Usage:
  kernl task create <title> [--title <t>] [--project <id>] [--status <status>] [--description <text>] [--tags <a,b>] [--due <YYYY-MM-DD>] [--json]

The title comes from the positional argument or --title, never both. An
unquoted multi-word positional title is joined into one string; the success
line quotes what was stored, so a swallowed word is visible.

Flags:
  --title <t>           The title, as an alternative to the positional form
  --project <id>        Attach the task to a project (creates the part_of edge)
  --status <status>     Initial status (server default when omitted)
  --description <text>  Long-form body
  --tags <a,b,c>        Comma-separated tags
  --due <YYYY-MM-DD>    Due date, a calendar day (no time, no timezone)
  --json                Emit {"id"} on stdout

Example:
  kernl task create "renew the domain" --project prj-1 --due 2026-08-01

### kernl task set
kernl task set — Change fields of an existing task

Usage:
  kernl task set <task-id> [--title <text>] [--status <status>] [--tags <a,b>] [--due <YYYY-MM-DD>] [--json]

At least one field is required. Only the flags you pass are touched;
passing an empty value clears the field: --tags "" removes every tag and
--due "" removes the due date.

Flags:
  --title <text>      New title (cannot be empty)
  --status <status>   New status (cannot be empty)
  --tags <a,b,c>      Replace the tag set; "" clears it
  --due <YYYY-MM-DD>  Replace the due date; "" clears it
  --json              Emit {"id","updated"} on stdout

### kernl task delete
kernl task delete — Delete a task and its companion note

Usage:
  kernl task delete <task-id> --yes [--json]

Destructive: removes the task node and the companion note that was
created with it, file included. Requires --yes; without it the task that
would be deleted is printed and nothing is sent to the server.

### kernl project
kernl project — Manage projects (list, create, edit, delete)

Usage:
  kernl project <list|create|set|delete> [args...]

Subcommands:
  list           List projects with status and task counts
  create         Create a project (and its companion note)
  set            Edit a project's title, description, status or tags
  delete         Delete a project and its companion note (requires --yes)

Projects are graph nodes with a companion note in the vault. These verbs
call the same REST API the web GUI calls, so a server must be running:
start one with 'kernl serve', or point elsewhere with --server <url>
(env: KERNL_SERVER).

Run 'kernl project <subcommand> --help' for details on each.

### kernl project list
kernl project list — List projects with status and task counts

Usage:
  kernl project list [--json]

Flags:
  --json  Emit the API's [{"id","title","description","status","tags",
          "createdAt","updatedAt","taskCount","doneCount"}] on stdout

### kernl project create
kernl project create — Create a project (and its companion note)

Usage:
  kernl project create [--json] [--description <text>] [--status <s>] [--tags <a,b>] <title>

The title is required; give it positionally or with --title.

Flags:
  --title <text>        Title, when you prefer a flag over the positional arg
  --description <text>  Free-text description
  --status <status>     Initial status (server-side default when omitted)
  --tags <a,b,c>        Comma-separated tags
  --json                Emit {"id"} on stdout

Example:
  kernl project create --tags home,infra "Rebuild the homelab backups"

### kernl project set
kernl project set — Edit a project's title, description, status or tags

Usage:
  kernl project set [--json] [--title <t>] [--description <d>] [--status <s>] [--tags <a,b>] <id>

Only the fields you pass are touched; at least one is required. Passing an
empty value clears the field ('--tags ""' removes every tag), which is why
omitting a flag and passing it empty are different things.

Flags:
  --title <text>        New title (may not be empty)
  --description <text>  New description ("" clears it)
  --status <status>     New status (may not be empty)
  --tags <a,b,c>        Replace the tag list ("" clears it)
  --json                Emit {"id","updated":true} on stdout

Example:
  kernl project set --status done fkq3v9

### kernl project delete
kernl project delete — Delete a project and its companion note (requires --yes)

Usage:
  kernl project delete [--json] <id> --yes

Destructive and not undoable: it removes the project node AND deletes the
companion note file from the vault. Tasks are not cascaded — they keep their
projectId and render as unassigned.

Without --yes nothing is deleted: the project that would go is printed and
the command exits 0.

Flags:
  --yes   Actually delete
  --json  Emit {"id","deleted":true} (or the preview) on stdout

### kernl note
kernl note — Read and write vault notes (the same files the editor edits)

Usage:
  kernl note <list|read|write|delete|tags|suggest|apply-hunks> [args...]

Subcommands:
  list           List notes with their graph node id, type and title
  read           Print a note's contents
  write          Create or overwrite a note from a file or stdin
  delete         Move a note to the trash (requires --yes)
  tags           List every note tag with the files carrying it
  suggest        Ask the DA for edit hunks on a note (never writes)
  apply-hunks    Apply hunks from 'note suggest' to a note

Every path is vault-relative (e.g. "projects/kernl.md"); the server
resolves it against vault.root and rejects anything that escapes it.

Run 'kernl note <subcommand> --help' for details on each.

### kernl note list
kernl note list — List notes with their graph node id, type and title

Usage:
  kernl note list [--files] [--json]

Flags:
  --files  List the .md files on disk instead (files the graph has not
           indexed yet show up here and nowhere else)
  --json   Emit the API response verbatim on stdout

### kernl note read
kernl note read — Print a note's contents

Usage:
  kernl note read <path> [--json]

Writes the file bytes to stdout unchanged, so it pipes.

Flags:
  --json  Emit {"path","content"} instead — the route itself returns
          text/plain, so there is no server JSON to pass through

### kernl note write
kernl note write — Create or overwrite a note from a file or stdin

Usage:
  kernl note write <path> [--file <local-path>] [--json]

The body comes from --file, or from stdin when --file is omitted.
Writing a .md note the server does not know yet gets a node id injected
into its frontmatter.

Flags:
  --file <local-path>  Read the body from a local file instead of stdin
  --json               Emit {"status":"saved"} on stdout

Examples:
  kernl note write inbox/idea.md --file ./draft.md
  echo "# Title" | kernl note write inbox/idea.md

### kernl note delete
kernl note delete — Move a note to the trash (requires --yes)

Usage:
  kernl note delete <path> --yes [--json]

Destructive: the file goes to the system trash and the vault watcher
reconciles its graph node away. Without --yes this prints what would be
deleted and exits 0.

Flags:
  --yes   Actually delete
  --json  Emit {"path","status"} on stdout

### kernl note tags
kernl note tags — List every note tag with the files carrying it

Usage:
  kernl note tags [--json]

Flags:
  --json  Emit {"<tag>":{"files":[...]}} on stdout

### kernl note suggest
kernl note suggest — Ask the DA for edit hunks on a note (never writes)

Usage:
  kernl note suggest <path> --instruction <text> [--json]

Returns line-aligned hunks to review; nothing is written until they are
fed back through 'kernl note apply-hunks'. Needs an llm section in
kernl.yaml — without one the server answers 503.

Flags:
  --instruction <text>  What to change (required)
  --json                Emit {"hunks":[{"id","from","to","content"}]}

### kernl note apply-hunks
kernl note apply-hunks — Apply hunks from 'note suggest' to a note

Usage:
  kernl note apply-hunks <path> [--hunks <local-path>] [--json]

Reads a JSON array of hunks — either the "hunks" array of a suggest
response, or the whole response object — from --hunks or stdin, and
applies exactly those. Pass only the hunks that were reviewed.

Flags:
  --hunks <local-path>  Read the hunks from a local file instead of stdin
  --json                Emit {"status","last_modified"} on stdout

Example:
  kernl note suggest daily.md --instruction "tighten the intro" --json > h.json
  kernl note apply-hunks daily.md --hunks h.json

### kernl inbox
kernl inbox — Triage the capture inbox (list, add, process, classify)

Usage:
  kernl inbox <list|add|process|convert|reopen|classify|auto-classify|prep|rollups|batch-log|batch> [args...]

Subcommands:
  list           List pending captures (or the processed ones)
  add            Add a capture through the running server (text arg or stdin)
  process        Turn a capture into a node (note, task, project, bookmark, discard)
  convert        Legacy single-action triage of a capture
  reopen         Undo a process: delete the derived node, requeue the capture
  classify       Run one LLM classification pass over the unclassified pending captures
  auto-classify  Read or flip the background auto-classify switch
  prep           Generate (or show) the DA briefing note for a capture
  rollups        Show captures grouped by the day they were created
  batch-log      Show the raw and final segments of an imported batch
  batch          Split one pasted export into many captures (analyze, preview, apply)

Every inbox verb talks to a running 'kernl serve' over the same REST API the
web inbox uses. 'kernl capture' is the offline path: it writes the graph
directly and needs no server.

A capture's body is never rewritten here — the CLI sends only what a capture
becomes (target, title, tags); the body travels untouched from the capture.

Run 'kernl inbox <subcommand> --help' for details on each.

### kernl inbox list
kernl inbox list — List pending captures (or the processed ones)

Usage:
  kernl inbox list [--processed] [--json]

Flags:
  --processed  List captures that already left the queue, and what they became
  --json       Emit the API response verbatim (camelCase)

### kernl inbox add
kernl inbox add — Add a capture through the running server (text arg or stdin)

Usage:
  kernl inbox add [--json] [--] <text> | echo <text> | kernl inbox add

Prints the new capture's ID; --json (first argument only) emits {"id"}.

Examples:
  kernl inbox add "call the accountant tomorrow"
  echo "idea: robot mode" | kernl inbox add
  kernl inbox add -- --help   (adds the literal text "--help")

### kernl inbox process
kernl inbox process — Turn a capture into a node (note, task, project, bookmark, discard)

Usage:
  kernl inbox process <capture-id> --target <note|task|project|bookmark|update|discard> [flags]

Flags:
  --target <t>        What the capture becomes (required)
  --title <text>      Title for the created node (default: the capture's own)
  --project-id <id>   File a task under an existing project
  --tags <a,b,c>      Comma-separated tags
  --due <YYYY-MM-DD>  Due date; on --target task only
  --link-to <id>      Link the created node to an existing node
  --target-note <id>  With --target update: the note to merge into
  --json              Emit the API response verbatim

One invocation sends one action. A capture that fans out into several nodes
at once is a GUI/API flow; here, reopen and process again.

### kernl inbox convert
kernl inbox convert — Legacy single-action triage of a capture

Usage:
  kernl inbox convert <capture-id> <note|task|project|bookmark|discard> [--json]

Kept because the API keeps it. Prefer 'kernl inbox process', which carries a
title, tags, a project and a due date.

### kernl inbox reopen
kernl inbox reopen — Undo a process: delete the derived node, requeue the capture

Usage:
  kernl inbox reopen <capture-id> --yes [--json]

Destructive: deletes the capture's derived node and requeues it. Requires
--yes. Without it nothing is written — the capture that would be reopened is
printed and the command exits 0.

Flags:
  --yes   Actually reopen
  --json  Emit {"id","reopened":true} (or the preview) on stdout

### kernl inbox classify
kernl inbox classify — Run one LLM classification pass over the unclassified pending captures

Usage:
  kernl inbox classify [--json]

Fails loud when no LLM provider is configured — Fix: set llm.provider in
kernl.yaml, then restart the server.

### kernl inbox auto-classify
kernl inbox auto-classify — Read or flip the background auto-classify switch

Usage:
  kernl inbox auto-classify [on|off] [--json]

With no argument it only reads the current state and reports whether an LLM
is configured at all. The switch is session-only: it resets to the config
default when the server restarts.

### kernl inbox prep
kernl inbox prep — Generate (or show) the DA briefing note for a capture

Usage:
  kernl inbox prep <capture-id> [--show] [--json]

Bare 'prep' is get-or-create: it returns the capture's existing prep note,
and only generates one when there is none. Generation costs one LLM call, so
the first prep of a given capture is the only invocation that spends anything
— every later one is a read, and re-running is free and idempotent.

'--show' is get-if-exists: it never generates. On an already-prepped capture
the two forms are identical.

Flags:
  --show  Read the existing prep note instead of generating one. When none
          exists this prints "no prep yet" and exits 0 — absence is an
          answer, not a bad invocation

Generating needs an LLM provider; --show does not.

### kernl inbox rollups
kernl inbox rollups — Show captures grouped by the day they were created

Usage:
  kernl inbox rollups [--json]

### kernl inbox batch-log
kernl inbox batch-log — Show the raw and final segments of an imported batch

Usage:
  kernl inbox batch-log <batch-id> [--json]

### kernl inbox batch
kernl inbox batch — Split one pasted export into many captures (analyze, preview, apply)

Usage:
  kernl inbox batch <analyze|preview|apply> [--file <path>] [--split <mode>] [--source <s>] [--title <text>] [--json]

Subcommands:
  analyze        Split the text and enrich it with an LLM (a title, merges to consider)
  preview        Split the text deterministically, with no LLM in the path
  apply          Create the captures (requires --yes)

Reads the text from --file, or from stdin when the flag is omitted.

Flags (all three subcommands):
  --file <path>   Local file to read instead of stdin
  --split <mode>  auto (default), whatsapp, lines, blocks, divider, markdown, semantic
  --source <s>    whatsapp or text; the server infers it when omitted
  --title <text>  Context title for the batch
  --json          Emit the API response verbatim

The pasted text is sent exactly as read. Capture bodies are rebuilt
server-side from that text, so neither this CLI nor a model behind it can
rewrite what you captured; merge decisions are a GUI review flow.

Run 'kernl inbox batch <subcommand> --help' for details on each.

### kernl inbox batch analyze
kernl inbox batch analyze — Split the text and enrich it with an LLM (a title, merges to consider)

Usage:
  kernl inbox batch analyze [--file <path>] [--split <mode>] [--source <s>] [--title <text>] [--json]

Needs an LLM provider on the server; 'preview' is the same split without one.
Writes nothing — it only reports how the text would divide.

### kernl inbox batch preview
kernl inbox batch preview — Split the text deterministically, with no LLM in the path

Usage:
  kernl inbox batch preview [--file <path>] [--split <mode>] [--source <s>] [--title <text>] [--json]

Writes nothing. Use it to check a --split mode before applying it.

### kernl inbox batch apply
kernl inbox batch apply — Create the captures (requires --yes)

Usage:
  kernl inbox batch apply [--file <path>] [--split <mode>] [--source <s>] [--title <text>] --yes [--json]

Without --yes nothing is created: the request is answered by the preview
route and the output says so, including under --json, which wraps the preview
in {"applied":false,"wouldApply":true,...}.

Flags:
  --yes  Actually create the captures

### kernl memory
kernl memory — Inspect and edit what the DA remembers (claims and telos)

Usage:
  kernl memory <topics|claims|telos|add-claim|refute> [args...]

Subcommands:
  topics         List the subjects that still have at least one active claim
  claims         List the active claims of one topic
  telos          Show the always-injected telos notes and their context footprint
  add-claim      Assert a claim yourself, without going through the DA
  refute         Refute a claim so it stops being retrieved

Talks to a running server over the REST API, so 'kernl serve' must be up
(or point elsewhere with --server <url> / KERNL_SERVER).

Memory has two halves: claims — statements the DA learned or you asserted,
retrieved by relevance — and telos, the notes tagged 'telos' that are always
injected into the DA's context.

Run 'kernl memory <subcommand> --help' for details on each.

### kernl memory topics
kernl memory topics — List the subjects that still have at least one active claim

Usage:
  kernl memory topics [--json]

A subject whose every claim was refuted is not listed.

Flags:
  --json  Emit {"topics":[...]} verbatim on stdout

### kernl memory claims
kernl memory claims — List the active claims of one topic

Usage:
  kernl memory claims --topic <topic> [--json]

Refuted claims are filtered out by the server. --topic is required:
the route has no "all topics" mode.

Flags:
  --topic <topic>  Which subject to read (list them with: kernl memory topics)
  --json           Emit {"claims":[...]} verbatim on stdout

### kernl memory telos
kernl memory telos — Show the always-injected telos notes and their context footprint

Usage:
  kernl memory telos [--json]

Prints every note tagged 'telos' plus the size of the block the chat
engine actually injects, so you can see when it is being truncated.

Flags:
  --json  Emit {"notes":[...],"injection":{...}} verbatim on stdout

### kernl memory add-claim
kernl memory add-claim — Assert a claim yourself, without going through the DA

Usage:
  kernl memory add-claim --subject <topic> <statement> [--json]

The claim is stored with user provenance, so it is distinguishable
from one the DA learned.

Flags:
  --subject <topic>  The subject the claim belongs to (required)
  --json             Emit {"id"} on stdout

Example:
  kernl memory add-claim --subject deploys "releases are cut from tags, never from master"

### kernl memory refute
kernl memory refute — Refute a claim so it stops being retrieved

Usage:
  kernl memory refute <claim-id> [--reason <text>] [--json]

The claim is not deleted: a refutation node is recorded against it and
the read paths stop returning it.

Flags:
  --reason <text>  Why it is wrong (kept with the refutation)
  --json           Emit {"status","id"} verbatim on stdout

### kernl graph
kernl graph — Read the knowledge graph (nodes, edges, search, relatedness)

Usage:
  kernl graph <nodes|search|related|briefing|edges> [args...]

Subcommands:
  nodes          List every live node in the graph
  search         Prefix-search node titles
  related        Show the nodes a node is related to
  briefing       Print the DA briefing note attached to a node
  edges          List every stored edge between live nodes

Every subcommand is read-only. They talk to a running server over the
REST API, so 'kernl serve' must be up (or point elsewhere with
--server <url> / KERNL_SERVER).

Run 'kernl graph <subcommand> --help' for details on each.

### kernl graph nodes
kernl graph nodes — List every live node in the graph

Usage:
  kernl graph nodes [--json]

The whole graph, tombstones excluded. The route takes no filters, so
narrow it with 'kernl graph search' rather than expecting flags here.

Flags:
  --json  Emit [{"id","title","type"}] verbatim on stdout

### kernl graph search
kernl graph search — Prefix-search node titles

Usage:
  kernl graph search <query> [--type <node-type>] [--limit <n>] [--json]

Matches title prefixes (this is the editor's wikilink autocomplete),
not full-text over bodies.

Flags:
  --type <node-type>  Restrict to one node type, e.g. note, task, project
  --limit <n>         Maximum hits, 1-50 (server default: 10; it clamps at 50)
  --json              Emit [{"id","title","type"}] verbatim on stdout

Example:
  kernl graph search backup --type note --limit 5

### kernl graph related
kernl graph related — Show the nodes a node is related to

Usage:
  kernl graph related <node-id> [--limit <n>] [--json]

Computed relevance, not stored edges — use 'kernl graph edges' for the
connections as persisted.

Flags:
  --limit <n>  Maximum results (server default: 10)
  --json       Emit [{"id","title","type"}] verbatim on stdout

### kernl graph briefing
kernl graph briefing — Print the DA briefing note attached to a node

Usage:
  kernl graph briefing <node-id> [--json]

A node the DA never briefed is a normal answer, not a broken
invocation: it exits 0 with "No briefing for <id> yet." (or {"briefing":null}
under --json), so branch on the body/JSON, never on the exit code.

Flags:
  --json  Emit {"id","title","body"} verbatim on stdout, or {"briefing":null}
          when the node has no briefing yet

### kernl graph edges
kernl graph edges — List every stored edge between live nodes

Usage:
  kernl graph edges [--json]

The edges table as persisted — the source of truth for checking that
connections are actually being made.

Flags:
  --json  Emit [{"id","src","dst","label"}] verbatim on stdout

### kernl settings
kernl settings — Read and write kernl.yaml through the running server

Usage:
  kernl settings <get|set> [args...]

Subcommands:
  get            Show the saved settings and which keys await a restart
  set            Update one section of kernl.yaml (llm, vault, inbox, runtime)

The same settings page the GUI exposes, from the shell. A server must be
running ('kernl serve'), or point elsewhere with --server <url> (env:
KERNL_SERVER).

Every write lands in the kernl.yaml the server was started with — nothing
is applied to the process that is already running. Fields you change stay
listed under "restart pending" until the server is restarted.

Run 'kernl settings <subcommand> --help' for details on each.

### kernl settings get
kernl settings get — Show the saved settings and which keys await a restart

Usage:
  kernl settings get [--json]

Reads the config file back from disk, so it shows what is persisted, not
what the running process holds. The LLM API key is never returned by the
server — only whether one is set.

Flags:
  --json  Emit the API's settings object verbatim (camelCase)

### kernl settings set
kernl settings set — Update one section of kernl.yaml (llm, vault, inbox, runtime)

Usage:
  kernl settings set <llm|vault|inbox|runtime> [--field <value>...] [--json]

Each section is written as a whole, so the current values are read first
and only the flags you pass are changed. At least one flag is required.

Saved to kernl.yaml, NOT applied to the running server: the process keeps
the values it booted with until you restart it. The output lists the keys
still waiting on that restart.

llm      --provider <openai|anthropic|ollama|noop>
         --model <name>            --endpoint <http(s) url>
         --api-key <key>           (never echoed back; omit to keep the stored one)
vault    --root <absolute dir>     (must already exist)
         --coalesce-window-ms <n>  --move-window-ms <n>
         --rescan-interval-sec <n>
inbox    --auto-prep <true|false>  --da-subdir <relative path inside the vault>
runtime  --server-port <1-65535>   --worktree-root <absolute path>
         --max-concurrent-beads <1-64>
         --run-state-path <absolute path>
         --stage-retry-attempts <0-10>
         --sweep-interval-sec <n>  --pr-stale-warn-days <n>
         --sweep-failure-limit <n> --sweep-backoff-minutes <5,15,60>

Flags:
  --json  Emit the API's full settings object after the write

Example:
  kernl settings set llm --provider openai --model gpt-4o-mini

### kernl health
kernl health — Check that the server is up and answering (no subcommand needed)

Usage:
  kernl health [update-check] [--json]

Subcommands:
  update-check   Ask the server whether a newer kernl is available

The verb to reach for when something is wrong: it says whether a server is
reachable at the address this CLI would use, and which vault that server
has open. Plain 'kernl health' does the check; the subcommand below is the
GUI's update banner.

When no server answers, the error names the address that was tried and how
to start one — that failure is the useful answer, not a crash.

Flags:
  --json  Emit the API's {"status","vaultRoot","vaultLabel"} verbatim

Run 'kernl health update-check' for the app-update check.

### kernl health update-check
kernl health update-check — Ask the server whether a newer kernl is available

Usage:
  kernl health update-check [--json]

Calls the same endpoint the GUI's update banner uses. That endpoint does
not contact any release feed yet — it answers {"status":"unknown","checked":false}.
Branch on "checked": false means "nobody looked", not "you are current".

Flags:
  --json  Emit the API's {"status","checked","detail"} verbatim

### kernl approval
kernl approval — See and resolve the gates waiting on a human

Usage:
  kernl approval <list|resolve> [args...]

Subcommands:
  list           Show what is waiting, for what, and since when
  resolve        Answer one approval (approve/reject, or an in-session prompt)

Approvals are the points where the orchestrator stops and waits for a
person. These verbs call the same REST API the web GUI calls, so a server
must be running: start one with 'kernl serve', or point elsewhere with
--server <url> (env: KERNL_SERVER).

Run 'kernl approval <subcommand> --help' for details on each.

### kernl approval list
kernl approval list — Show what is waiting, for what, and since when

Usage:
  kernl approval list [--json]

Actionable requests are listed first, oldest first, each with the exact
command that resolves it.

Flags:
  --json  Emit the API's approval array verbatim (camelCase)

### kernl approval resolve
kernl approval resolve — Answer one approval (approve/reject, or an in-session prompt)

Usage:
  kernl approval resolve <approval-id> --action <action> [--session <session-id>] [--yes] [--json]

Without --session the request goes to the orchestrator gate, whose actions
are: approve, reject.

With --session it answers the prompt an agent raised inside that terminal
session, whose actions are: accept, always_approve, decline. 'always_approve'
also silences future prompts of the same shape for that session.

Granting an approval (approve, accept, always_approve) requires --yes:
letting an agent proceed unreviewed is the one mistake this gate exists to
prevent. Declining does not, since it only stops work. Without --yes nothing
is sent to the server and the command exits 2 — a refused mutation is not a
success.

Flags:
  --action <action>  Required. What to answer
  --session <id>     Answer inside that terminal session instead
  --yes              Confirm a granting action
  --json             Emit {"id","resolved":true} (or the server's body)

Example:
  kernl approval list
  kernl approval resolve apr-7 --action approve --yes
  kernl approval resolve apr-7 --action decline --session sess-2

### kernl session
kernl session — Poke a running agent session

Usage:
  kernl session <nudge|nudge-prompts> [args...]

Subcommands:
  nudge          Send a prompt into a session that has gone quiet
  nudge-prompts  Show the prompt text each preset would send

A session is one agent run driving one bead. These verbs call the same REST
API the web GUI calls, so a server must be running: start one with
'kernl serve', or point elsewhere with --server <url> (env: KERNL_SERVER).

Live session output is an SSE stream and has no CLI verb; read it in the GUI
or with 'curl -N <server>/api/sessions/<id>/events'.

Run 'kernl session <subcommand> --help' for details on each.

### kernl session nudge
kernl session nudge — Send a prompt into a session that has gone quiet

Usage:
  kernl session nudge <session-id> [--preset <preset>] [--prompt <text>] [--json]

Delivers a message to the agent already running in that session. Use it when
a run stalls, or to push it to record its status.

Flags:
  --preset <preset>  generic (default, server-side) or advance_status
  --prompt <text>    Send this exact text instead of the preset's template
  --json             Emit the API's {"status","sessionId"} on stdout

A session that is mid-turn answers 409 and a session with no agent attached
answers 422; both exit 2, since the fix is to retry or pick another session.

Example:
  kernl session nudge sess-2 --preset advance_status

### kernl session nudge-prompts
kernl session nudge-prompts — Show the prompt text each preset would send

Usage:
  kernl session nudge-prompts <session-id> [--json]

Prints the templates already substituted for this session's bead and repo,
so you can read what 'session nudge' would send before sending it — or copy
one, edit it, and pass it back with --prompt.

Flags:
  --json  Emit the API's {"beadId","opencodeSessionId","running","generic",
          "advance_status"} on stdout

### kernl ingest
kernl ingest — Feed documents into the graph and triage what they extracted

Usage:
  kernl ingest <paste|upload|source|trigger|queue> [args...]

Subcommands:
  paste          Ingest text passed as an argument or piped on stdin
  upload         Ingest a local .md or .txt file (sent as multipart/form-data)
  source         Fetch a URL and ingest what it returns
  trigger        Ingest a file already inside the vault, by server-side path
  queue          Read and resolve the ingest review queue

Ingest talks to a running 'kernl serve' and needs an LLM provider on the
server: extraction is an LLM pass. Without one every route answers 503 and
the CLI surfaces that verbatim — Fix: set llm.provider in kernl.yaml, then
restart the server.

Extraction runs in the background: paste, upload, source and trigger return
as soon as the content is staged. What they produced shows up in
'kernl ingest queue list'.

Run 'kernl ingest <subcommand> --help' for details on each.

### kernl ingest paste
kernl ingest paste — Ingest text passed as an argument or piped on stdin

Usage:
  kernl ingest paste [--title <text>] [--json] [--] <text> | cat f.md | kernl ingest paste

Flags:
  --title <text>  Becomes a leading H1 so the extractor can anchor on it
  --json          Emit the API response verbatim

Everything after '--' is text, even when it looks like a flag.

### kernl ingest upload
kernl ingest upload — Ingest a local .md or .txt file (sent as multipart/form-data)

Usage:
  kernl ingest upload <path> [--json]

The file is read locally and uploaded whole; the server rejects anything
that is not .md or .txt, is empty, is not UTF-8, or exceeds 2 MiB.

### kernl ingest source
kernl ingest source — Fetch a URL and ingest what it returns

Usage:
  kernl ingest source <url> [--kind <kind>] [--title <text>] [--json]

Flags:
  --kind <kind>   How the fetcher should read the URL (server decides by default)
  --title <text>  Override the title the fetcher derived
  --json          Emit {"sourceNodeId","title","kind"} verbatim

A bookmark node is created for the source, so the extracted notes stay
traceable to where they came from.

### kernl ingest trigger
kernl ingest trigger — Ingest a file already inside the vault, by server-side path

Usage:
  kernl ingest trigger <file-path> [--node <node-id>] [--json]

The path is resolved by the server, not by this shell: it must be readable
by the 'kernl serve' process. To send a local file's contents instead, use
'kernl ingest upload'.

Flags:
  --node <id>  Attach the extraction to an existing source node

### kernl ingest queue
kernl ingest queue — Read and resolve the ingest review queue

Usage:
  kernl ingest queue <list|resolve|merge-plan> [args...]

Subcommands:
  list           List the pending reviews: id, proposed action, title
  resolve        Apply a review: create a page, merge into one, or discard it
  merge-plan     Ask the LLM which hunks an Update would add

Hunk-by-hunk merge review is a GUI flow. 'resolve --action update' from the
CLI links the source to the target note and clears the review without
rewriting the note's body.

Run 'kernl ingest queue <subcommand> --help' for details on each.

### kernl ingest queue list
kernl ingest queue list — List the pending reviews: id, proposed action, title

Usage:
  kernl ingest queue list [--json]

Flags:
  --json  Emit the API's review array verbatim (camelCase)

### kernl ingest queue resolve
kernl ingest queue resolve — Apply a review: create a page, merge into one, or discard it

Usage:
  kernl ingest queue resolve <id> --action <create-page|update|discard> [--target-note <id>] [--json]

Flags:
  --action <a>        create-page, update or discard — REQUIRED, no default.
                      'discard' deletes the review permanently; there is no undo.
  --target-note <id>  With --action update: the note to merge into
  --json              Emit the API response verbatim

There is no default action: an omitted --action used to mean "discard", so a
forgotten flag destroyed the review it was resolving.

### kernl ingest queue merge-plan
kernl ingest queue merge-plan — Ask the LLM which hunks an Update would add

Usage:
  kernl ingest queue merge-plan <id> [--json]

Reads only — it plans the merge without applying it. An empty target means
there is no confident note to merge into; resolve with --action create-page.
